system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 9: Appendix 2 - Fingerstyle Training
Pages 286-317 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Introduction to Fingerstyle Guitar

Fingerstyle technique will open up a whole world of creative possibilities for your jazz guitar playing.

This technique allows you to easily play multiple voices at once on one guitar – enabling you to create
complex chord melody arrangements and chord solos.

Note: you don’t need a particular type of guitar for fingerstyle. This plucking technique can be used
on steel string, nylon string, electric and acoustic instruments.


Finger Naming

Throughout this section on fingerstyle exercises, you’ll notice lettering like this on the diagrams:




These letters stand for the five picking hand fingers, based on the Spanish names for each finger:

   p = thumb (“pulgar” in Spanish)
   i = index (“indice” in Spanish)
   m = middle (“medio” in Spanish)
   a = ring finger (“anular” in Spanish)
   c = pinky finger (“chiquito” in Spanish)




(This is the same naming convention as used by classical guitarists).

In the above diagram, I’ve indicated to play the exercise with alternating i and m fingers, then once
you are comfortable with that try with p and i. The ‘sim’ means to continue the finger pattern for the
exercise.

It’s very important for fingerstyle that, right from the start, you adopt a hand position that
promotes agility and freedom of movement.

Doing this will set you up for success later on as you develop this technique.


Tips for Correct Fingerstyle Hand Placement

   Keep the wrist naturally curved in your plucking hand – avoid flatting or overarching the wrist too
   much.
   Keep the wrist in alignment with the arm, avoid kinking the wrist at an angle (it’s ok to strike the
   strings slightly diagonally).
   Keep your hand relaxed as possible, take frequent breaks while you are still learning these
   techniques.
   Fingernails can be helpful for getting more depth and tone of the sound, but is entirely optional
   (especially on electric guitar).

Now that you’re up to speed with the names of each finger and the key points on good fingerstyle
technique, now head to the next lesson for some essential fingerstyle workouts.


Rest Stroke vs Free Stroke

Now that you’ve covered the correct hand and arm placement for fingerstyle technique, in this lesson
you’re going to have a closer look at the picking motion of the fingers themselves.

There are two methods of plucking the string with fingerstyle:

   “Rest stroke” – when you play a string and then rest your finger on the string behind the one
   you plucked at the end of the motion.

   “Free stroke” – when you pluck the string and let your finger fly “freely” above the other strings
   as you end the motion.

Let’s do some exercises which will work out your fingerstyle technique!




Walking Finger Workouts

“Walking finger” exercises are a great way to build overall coordination and speed for the plucking
hand.

They involve playing basic patterns whilst alternating either the i and m fingers, or i and a fingers.

Note: Although it can feel initially more awkward, I personally have found I get much more speed
with alternating i and a, rather than i and m. But everyone’s hands are different, so practice both
combinations for now – you decide on your main picking combination down the track.

The following exercises are straightforward. But – even if you are already familiar with fingerstyle use
these as an opportunity to check that you have a good hand and arm position and that you have
optimal plucking motion of the fingers.



 Task: practice one of the walking finger workouts daily for 5 minutes until you feel the alternating
 finger motion becoming smooth and easy. Master each exercise before moving to the next.


Walking Exercise #1 – Walking Fingers (4 Plucks per String on Am Chord)



 Play audio example A2-1


Walking Exercise #2 – Walking Fingers (2 Plucks per String)



 Play audio example A2-2




Walking Exercise #3 – G Major Scale



 Play audio example A2-3


Fingerstyle String Crossing

In many ways, string crossing is easier for fingerstyle compared to using a plectrum. Because you
essentially have five “plectrums” (i.e. fingers) on one hand, grabbing a note on an adjacent string is
not much of an issue.

However, because the length of each finger is different, sometimes it’s awkward to alternate fingers
while crossing strings.

The following exercise is excellent for developing your ability to get comfortable with string crossing
while alternating fingers.



 Task: practice The Moonwalker string crossing exercise daily for 5 minutes, until you feel it
 becomes natural and easy to play.



Fingerstyle String Crossing Exercise – The Moonwalker



   Play audio example A2-4


Once you’re familiar with the previous basic walking finger exercises, transition to practicing just this
one.

Why?

It simultaneously works out walking finger technique and string crossing all at once – saving you
practice time.

Although rest stroke is a good first stroke to learn to gain security with your plucking, at this point I
would recommend focusing on free stroke as your main picking technique.

In general, free stroke is a more much more useful for jazz guitar than rest stroke.


Using the Thumb

For a typical fingerstyle jazz guitarist, the thumb is generally reserved for playing bass notes in
chords or to play basslines alongside melodies on the higher strings.

(Wes Montgomery used to exclusively use his thumb for everything picking-wise. But in this case,
Wes was more using his thumb as a plectrum substitute rather than a typical fingerstyle technique.)

The motion should come from the lowest “3rd” joint of the thumb, with a small circular motion as you
pluck – similar to the motion of a paddle on a rowing boat.

Let’s get into some exercises to get our thumb fit and agile. Ensure that your hands feel relaxed and
free from tension and that you’ve adopted a good hand position as you play each exercise.



 Task: practice one of the thumb exercises daily for 5 minutes, until you feel the motion of the
 thumb becoming smooth and easy. Master each exercise before moving to the next.



Thumb Exercise #1 – G Major Scale

This is a basic exercise over a standard scale. Feel free to practice this technique over other scales as
well.

Although the thumb is usually reserved for playing bass notes, it’s still a good idea to practice thumb
strokes over all strings, as there are situations where you’ll need to use the thumb like this from time
to time.




   Play audio example A2-5


Thumb Exercise #2 – Bossa Nova Bassline

This basic bossa nova exercise is very useful for training the thumb to find each of the bass strings.




   Play audio example A2-6




Thumb Exercise #3 – Blues Walking Bass

This is the situation in which you would use the thumb most often the jazz guitar.

Feel free to improvise your own walking bass lines if you already have familiarity with doing so.




   Play audio example A2-7

Thumb Exercise #4 – Montuno Pattern

Here’s a classic Latin pattern that makes for a great thumb workout.

Most of the time, you’ll find yourself playing the thumb in combination with other fingers like this, so
it’s a good idea to get used to this technique at this point.




   Play audio example A2-8


Two-String Exercises

In this and the following lessons, we’re going to build upon what you’ve covered so far and do a deep
dive on plucking across multiple strings.

These are very important techniques to learn as a jazz guitarist, as they will enable you to play
intricate chord melody arrangements with multiple moving voices.


Tips for Plucking Multiple Strings

Ensure that your wrist is rolled slightly more towards the headstock to allow more space between the
index finger and thumb. If your hand position is incorrect, these two fingers will obstruct each other’s
motion when those fingers pluck notes together.




 The main ways to pluck adjacent strings is with the p and i fingers, or i and m fingers.




Regardless of which fingers you use, ensure that both fingers sound the notes simultaneously.




 For 3 notes at once, it’s preferable to use p i m, or i m a, so it’s worth practicing both these
 combinations.




I rarely use the little finger (c) on the right hand with fingerstyle technique – I tend to stick to 4-note
voicings as a maximum which means you will only need to use your four strongest fingers – p i m a.

Now that you’ve got familiarity with the basic movements that you need to do as a fingerstyle player
when picking multiple strings, let’s get to work with the following exercises which will build your
technique in this area step by step.

For most of these exercises, I suggest you try another plucking finger combination on the repeats to
help work out more picking combinations.



 Task: practice one of the two string exercises daily for 5 minutes until fluent. Master each
 exercise before moving to the next.


Two-String Exercise #1 – Two-String Shuffle

This exercise is an excellent starting point for your two-string workouts.

As I mentioned earlier, be conscious of the position and angle of your plucking hand as you play, as
well as the motion of your fingers.

Using a mirror to see what your hand is doing when you’re developing techniques like these is very
useful to check you’re doing things correctly.




   Play audio example A2-9




Two-String Exercise #2 – The Climber

This exercise is similar to the last one but with faster position shifts in the fretting hand.

You get to “kill two birds with one stone” with an exercise like this – i.e. working out your picking
technique and your position shifting at the same time.




   Play audio example A2-10


Two-String Exercise #3 – Tricks with Sixths

This exercise is the same as exercise #1 but uses 6ths intervals instead of 3rds.

Now you need to negotiate a string skip in the plucking hand, hence the use of pm and ia as marked.




   Play audio example A2-11




Two-String Exercise #4 – Louie The Leaper

Another exercise using 6ths.

I often incorporate 6ths moving horizontally across the fretboard like this in my solos, as they create
an interesting texture when improvising.




   Play audio example A2-12


Two-String Exercise #5 – Metheny 3rds

This is a useful pattern for developing finger independence in the plucking hand across 2 strings.

Notice that the thumb plays a note twice in a row. It’s quite natural for the thumb to do a double
pluck in these situations as it simplifies the picking fingering greatly.

The exercise sounds like something I saw Pat Metheny do once during a solo, hence the name.




   Play audio example A2-13


Two-String Exercise #6 – Slippery 6ths

This final exercise in this section is a variation on the previous exercise, using 6ths instead of 3rds
with the same picking pattern.

This line is a useful idea for solos and compositions and can help you think more horizontally in your
soloing too.




   Play audio example A2-14




It’s time to take things to the next level. Once you’ve mastered the exercises on this page, head to
the next lesson where you’re going to add some more fingers into the mix with multiple string
plucking techniques.


Three-String Exercises

Now that you’ve covered plucking two strings, let’s add more strings to the mix!

In this lesson, you’re going to look at working out your picking hand while you tackle negotiating
three strings.

The following patterns you’re going to learn are very useful for comping and solo guitar chord melody,
so they are essential exercises to know for that reason.

If you find these exercises a challenge, it would be worth revising the general tips on hand position
and finger motion covered in the first lesson of this module. Remember to keep your hand as relaxed
as possible.



 Task: practice these three string exercises daily for 5 minutes until fluent. Master each exercise
 before moving to the next.



Three-String Exercise #1 – Echoes of Cordoba

Here’s an exercise to get you started plucking 3 strings simultaneously – make sure all notes sound at
exactly the same time on each chord.

This exercise will also give your position shifting a good workout too. It reminds me a bit of
“Cordoba”, a classical piece by Albéniz.




   Play audio example A2-15


Three-String Exercise #2 – The Don Andrews

Here’s an exercise in homage to my wonderful guitar teacher now sadly passed away, Don Andrews.

Don had an exercise like this in one of his books and I used to practice it for 10 minutes a day for
months.

This exercise is excellent for developing cross-string arpeggio picking technique. It’s a neat texture
that can sound particularly good in solo guitar arrangements.

Try this picking pattern over other chord voicings too.




   Play audio example A2-16


Three-String Exercise #3 – Barry’s Burger

This is a good exercise to play because it’s a less predictable picking pattern in the plucking hand.

This will give you more flexibility in applying these techniques to your comping and chord melodies.




   Play audio example A2-17


Three-String Exercise #4 – Jazzy Giuliani

Back in my classical guitar studies at university, I used to practice 120 plucking hand exercises by
Mauro Giuliani, daily. It took nearly 2 hours to do all of them.

The worst part – the chord progression for every one of these exercises went from C to G7. Every
single one.

In the words of Scott Tennant, it was nothing short of “cruel and unusual punishment”.

So here’s one of the Giuliani picking patterns, jazzed up with more interesting harmony. Out of all the
120 Giuliani exercises, this is one of the most useful.

It’s a great way to develop thumb independence in a picking pattern context – the thumb moves
between the 5th and 4th strings as you play the exercise.




   Play audio example A2-18


Four-String Exercises

La pièce de résistance! Now that you’ve worked out three-string picking, let’s try four strings.

The technique and feeling in the fingers are very similar to the three-string exercises – you’re just
adding another plucking finger into the game.



 Task: practice these four-string exercises daily for 5 minutes until fluent. Master each exercise
 before moving to the next.



Four-String Exercise #1 – Danny Dreamer 1

Here’s a cool-sounding progression plucked using 4 fingers simultaneously.

Again, ensure that you’re sounding all the notes in each chord at the same time.




   Play audio example A2-19


Four-String Exercise #2 – Danny Dreamer 2

Here’s a variation of the above “Danny the Dreamer” exercise, using sequential plucking instead of
block chords.




   Play audio example A2-20


Four-String Exercise #3 Southern Land

This short arpeggio study is similar to the previous exercise in terms of the picking pattern used.

However the pattern is now twice as fast, incorporating 16th notes, so this is a more advanced
workout.




   Play audio example A2-21


Fingerstyle Etudes

To cap this appendix off, learn the following etudes in order to consolidate everything you’ve learned
so far in these fingerstyle lessons.

These etudes are based on common jazz guitar comping patterns that serve as great warmups for
working out your fingerstyle chops.

They are also handy to incorporate into chord melodies.


Etude 1 - Walking Bass Bb Blues

This first etude is a walking bass study over a Bb blues.

Although this sort of study is quite well known, it’s brilliant for gaining control over plucking multiple
strings.



 Task: Practice this study one bar at a time, until you can play it all the way through.




   Play audio example A2-22

Etude 2 - Bossa Nova Comping Etude

This is another useful exercise that I recommend to consolidate your multiple-strings technique.

This exercise is a great workout for fingerstyle technique and enables you to quickly gain facility in
the plucking hand as well as the independence of the thumb.

The exercise comprises a straightforward ii V I sequence, moving down two frets at a time.

I would suggest practicing the picking pattern over a single chord for a while until you get it under
your fingers first. After that, start to build the pattern over the full chord progression.



 Task: get started with this exercise by working out how to change the chords with the fretting
 hand. Once confident, build the picking pattern in the plucking hand step-by-step.




   Play audio example A2-23
